range.nativeRange: Eastern North America — Nova Scotia to Minnesota south to Georgia; native throughout Pennsylvania; commonly found in rich, moist, deciduous woodland floors

usNativeStatus.description: Native to the eastern US — from the Appalachian Mountains north to the Great Lakes region and New England; range extends from Georgia north to Quebec and west to Iowa and Minnesota; a woodland plant of rich, moist, deciduous forest; not native to the western US

invasive.description: Not invasive; a native woodland ephemeral; spreads very slowly by seed and division; overharvesting in the wild is the documented concern, not invasiveness

description.height: Leaves 6-9" (spring ephemeral foliage); flowers 8-12" (appear after leaves die back)
description.spread: Very slow spread by bulb offset; a well-established colony takes decades to form; this is a long-term planting that builds character and value over time
description.spacing: 4-6" apart; plant in large groups for harvest potential
description.growthRate: Slow — very slow; ramps require patience; colonies are measured in decades, not years
description.timeToMaturity: Leaves harvestable from year 1-2 (lightly); harvestable bulbs take 5-7 years; never overharvest — a colony's recovery is slow
description.lifespan: Long-lived perennial bulb; essentially permanent in suitable habitat
description.deciduousEvergreen: Spring ephemeral — one of the most early-emerging plants in the spring garden; leaves appear in March-April before the forest canopy leafs out; leaves die back by June; flowers appear on bare stems in June-July; completely dormant by July
description.winterInterest: None — dormant from July to March

lightAndWater.sun: Deep shade; requires the shade of a deciduous canopy; does NOT tolerate full sun (the leaves will sunburn and fail); the timing of ramps is specifically adapted to the brief window before the forest canopy leafs out; in the paw paw guild, the shading is ideal
lightAndWater.water: Medium; consistent moisture during the spring active season is essential; tolerates drier conditions during summer dormancy (which is convenient — it goes dormant just as summer dryness begins)

drainagePreference.description: Moist, well-drained; native to rich, moist, forest floors; tolerates clay-loam with good organic matter; does not tolerate waterlogged conditions

claySoilPerformance.description: Poor; ramps require rich, moist, well-structured woodland soil with high organic matter; heavy clay lacks the loose, humus-rich texture of their native forest floor habitat; clay inhibits the bulb development and drainage needed; deep compost amendment or woodland bed creation is required; not a clay-tolerant plant

soil.texturePreference: Rich, moist, humus-rich soil; leaf-litter-amended clay-loam is appropriate; the key factor is high organic matter content — replicate the woodland floor
soil.rootCharacter: True bulb with narrow leaves; small offsets form adjacent to the parent bulb to slowly expand the colony; bulbs are elongated and narrow (not round like garlic)

bloom.bloomTime: June-July (after leaves die back); the flowers appear on bare stems after the foliage has completely disappeared — a confusing and interesting trait
bloom.bloomDescription: Small, rounded clusters of tiny white flowers on bare 8-12" stems; individually modest but attractive
bloom.fragrance: Strongly garlicky-onion fragrance from leaves and bulbs; one of the most pungent members of the Allium family
bloom.pollination: Bees; the flowers are pollinated by insects seeking late-June/July pollen; modest ecological contribution during bloom

yield.edibleParts: Leaves (the primary spring harvest — harvest just 1-2 leaves per plant; the bright green, intensely flavored leaves are one of the most prized wild foods of the eastern US; use raw in salads, wilted as greens, in soups, pickled, or mixed into butter; the flavor is intensely garlicky-leek, complex, and unique; the spring ramp season is a cultural event in Appalachian communities); bulbs (can be harvested but this kills the plant — only harvest bulbs from the edge of a well-established, large colony, and never more than 10%; the bulbs are intensely flavored and excellent pickled or used as garlic/shallot substitute)
yield.harvestSeason: Leaves March-May (the brief spring window before die-back); bulbs May (when digging is possible but should be done sparingly)
yield.medicinalUses: Traditional Cherokee, Ojibwe, and Iroquois medicinal use — antiseptic, circulatory support, digestive, cold and fever treatment; the same allicin compounds as garlic with potentially more complex aromatics; considered a spring tonic in Appalachian tradition; one of the most important native medicinal food plants
yield.craftOtherYield: Pickled ramp bulbs (a spring specialty and excellent preserve); ramp salt; ramp butter; fermented ramps

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Non-toxic; edible; widely and enthusiastically eaten
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: TOXIC to dogs and cats — all Allium species cause hemolytic anemia; the same caution applies here; never feed ramps to dogs, cats, or horses; this is the standard Allium caution

roles: Food (highly prized wild edible — one of the most culturally and culinarily valued native plants of the eastern US)
Spring Ephemeral (provides spring ground cover before the canopy leafs out, then dies back to allow summer light to other plants)
companionPlants: Asimina triloba (paw paw — the natural woodland canopy companion)
Asarum canadense (wild ginger — same woodland floor community; ramps emerge early and die back; wild ginger fills the space in summer)
Matteuccia struthiopteris (ostrich fern — the ferns emerge as the ramps die back; a natural succession)
antagonists: Sun and drought — the two conditions that will kill ramps; these are not garden companions but site condition requirements

wildlife.attracts: Bees (modest June bloom); the early spring emergence provides a food source for early emerging insects before many other plants are active
wildlife.nestingHabitat: The underground bulbs provide some food storage for small mammals; not a primary nesting habitat

deer.description: Excellent — wild ramps are avoided by deer; the pungent garlic scent deters browsing; one of the few woodland plants that deer typically leave alone

salt.description: Poor — sensitive to salt; natural woodland habitat

drought.description: Poor during spring growing season; fine during summer dormancy

tolerances.other: Requires deep shade; slow growth is the primary limitation for garden use; sustainable harvest practices are essential — ramp populations in the wild have been overharvested in many areas; grow your own and harvest conservatively

maintenance.pruningNotes: No pruning needed. Harvest leaves gently — take one leaf per plant, cut rather than pull, and never take more than 20% of the colony's foliage. Allow the colony to grow undisturbed for the first 3-5 years before any significant harvest. Mark the location clearly — the plants disappear by July and it is easy to inadvertently dig the bulbs when working in the area.
propagationMethods: Seed (requires double dormancy stratification — warm + cold + warm; germinates in the second spring after sowing; very slow; viable but requires 2-3 years before any harvest)
bulb offsets (carefully separate offset bulbs from established plants in fall; replant immediately; this is the preferred propagation method)

cultivarNotes: No named cultivars; the straight species is always grown. Two botanical varieties are recognized: A. tricoccum var. tricoccum (the common form, broader leaves) and A. tricoccum var. burdickii (syn. A. burdickii, narrower leaves, slightly different flavor) — both forms are desirable. Plant whichever is available from a reputable native plant source.

gardenUse.designPlacement: Woodland garden, shaded food forest floor, native plant understory
gardenUse.acquisitionStatus: To source (native plant nursery; ensure sustainably propagated stock — wild-dug ramps are an ecological concern)

pennsylvaniaHegins.nativeToSite: Yes — Allium tricoccum is native to Pennsylvania; naturally occurs in moist, rich woodland throughout the state; potentially present in nearby woodland
pennsylvaniaHegins.paInvasiveStatus: Not invasive in PA; actually of conservation concern in some areas due to overharvesting
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Hegins red clay: significant amendment required — deep incorporation of leaf mold, compost, and woody material to create a woodland floor texture; or site in existing naturally shaded, humus-rich areas if any are present on the property
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Hegins: deep woodland compost amendment essential; clay soil is not naturally compatible with ramps
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: Allium tricoccum var. tricoccum is native to Zone 6a Pennsylvania; fully cold-hardy; the challenge is habitat creation, not cold hardiness
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Paw paw guild understory — listed as a companion plant in the paw paw guild design; shaded, moist area with woodland soil amendment; planted in drifts beneath the paw paw trees and spicebush; the spring ephemeral timing of ramps (leaves in March-May, dormant in summer) is perfectly complementary to the paw paw guild's ecology; the ramps provide early spring harvest before the paw paw canopy leafs out, then retire underground as the summer canopy takes over; combined with wild ginger and ostrich fern, the colony creates a layered, self-managing ground ecology; start with a significant initial planting (50+ bulbs if possible) to have a harvest-ready colony within 5-7 years
